message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Clear speech (deliberate hyperarticulation) produces a measurable intelligibility advantage over conversational speech for consonants/fricatives, with intelligibility benefits in prior literature ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points; in this study clear speech significantly lowered SNR identification thresholds for both normal-hearing and simulated-hearing-impaired listeners."

**Source:** https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to “conversational” speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

**(2) SNR identification thresholds:**

**(3) Focus on consonants/fricatives:**
The research emphasizes examination of consonantal features, particularly fricatives, as key acoustic elements distinguishing clear from conversational speech patterns.

I found relevant search results for your query. Here's what I found:

Maniwa, M., Jongman, A., and Wade, T. (2007) conducted research titled "Acoustic and perceptual properties of clearly produced fricatives," published as an unpublished doctor's dissertation at The University of Kansas, Lawrence, KS.

The research appears to have been later published as a journal article. A 2008 publication by Kazumi Maniwa, Allard Jongman, and Travis Wade appeared in The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America, volume 123, issue 2, pages 1114–1125.

Regarding the research focus, the study investigated whether clear speech improves the intelligibility of fricative consonants specifically, and how its effects on fricative perception might differ depending on listener population. The results showed that clear speech facilitated listeners' perception of place of articulation distinctions, as lower SNR thresholds for identification were observed for clear, relative to conversational, fricatives.

The server returned HTTP 403 Forbidden.

The response body was not retrieved. If this URL requires authentication, use an authenticated tool (e.g. `gh` for GitHub, or an MCP-provided fetch tool) instead of WebFetch.

# Study Findings on Clear Speech and Fricative Perception

**Full Abstract:**
"Speakers may adapt the phonetic details of their productions when they anticipate perceptual difficulty or comprehension failure on the part of a listener. Previous research suggests that a speaking style known as clear speech is more intelligible overall than casual, conversational speech for a variety of listener populations. However, it is unknown whether clear speech improves the intelligibility of fricative consonants specifically, or how its effects on fricative perception might differ depending on listener population. The primary goal of this study was to determine whether clear speech enhances fricative intelligibility for normal-hearing listeners and listeners with simulated impairment. Two experiments measured babble signal-to-noise ratio thresholds for fricative minimal pair distinctions for 14 normal-hearing listeners and 14 listeners with simulated sloping, recruiting impairment. Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall. However, for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were not found for non-sibilant pairs. Correlation analyses comparing acoustic and perceptual data indicated that a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners. Correlations between acoustic and perceptual data were less consistent for listeners with simulated impairment, and suggested that lower-frequency information may play a role."

**Key Findings:**

- **SNR Thresholds:** "Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall."
- **Both Listener Groups:** The research explicitly examined fricative perception in both normal-hearing and simulated hearing-impaired populations, finding that clear speech benefits applied broadly, though with limitations for impaired listeners on certain fricative pairs.
- **Focus:** The study concentrates specifically on fricative consonants and their intelligibility across listener populations.

content.text: The PubMed abstract confirms the core findings. Let me verify the "7 to 38 percentage points" range, which is the specific quantitative claim attributed to prior literature in the Interspeech 2007 paper.

The ranges documented in these studies vary depending on the specific conditions, listener populations, and languages studied, but generally fall within lower values (around 4-7 points) to higher values (around 23-26 points). The WebFetch on the primary PDF returned a slightly paraphrased version but did confirm the "7 to 38 percentage points" figure. The supporting quote provided in the claim matches the paper's introduction citing prior literature [3, 4, 5]. The PubMed abstract independently confirms the SNR threshold finding for both listener groups.

content.text:
The exact wording is confirmed directly from the primary source PDF text. The verbatim sentence reads:

"...resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to 'conversational' speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points."

This exactly matches the supporting quote in the claim. The grep also reveals an important nuance worth noting: the very next sentence in the paper states "However, some results call into question the robustness of the 'clear speech' [effect]" and references a study that observed "negative clear-speech benefits" in elderly hearing-impaired listeners for some vowels. This actually appears in the same source, but it concerns the general robustness caveat, not the consonant/fricative claim being made here.

Now let me assess each part of the claim against the evidence:

1. **"7 to 38 percentage points" for prior literature** — Verified verbatim in the primary source PDF text.
2. **"Clear speech significantly lowered SNR identification thresholds for both normal-hearing and simulated hearing-impaired listeners"** — Confirmed by the published JASA abstract: "Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall." The PubMed/JASA abstract states clear speech enhanced fricative intelligibility (lower babble SNR thresholds) for both groups.
3. **Focus on consonants/fricatives** — Confirmed; the study is specifically about fricative perception.

One caveat: the claim says clear speech "significantly lowered SNR identification thresholds for both" groups. The abstract qualifies this: "for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were NOT found for non-sibilant pairs." So the benefit for impaired listeners was overall but not universal across all fricative pairs. The claim says "for consonants/fricatives" generally and "for both" groups, which the abstract supports at the overall level ("helped both groups overall"). The claim does not overstate it as universal across all pairs, so this is a minor qualification rather than a contradiction.

The claim is well-supported, the source is a peer-reviewed Interspeech/ISCA paper (later published in JASA), and the figures are quoted verbatim. The age (2007/2008) is not a problem because this is foundational acoustic-phonetics science about clear speech, not a fast-moving benchmark field.

input.evidence: VERIFIED against primary source. Extracted the exact text from the Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2007) Interspeech PDF (isca-archive.org). The verbatim sentence reads: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to 'conversational' speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points." This matches the claim's '7 to 38 percentage points' figure exactly. The SNR-threshold finding is independently confirmed by the peer-reviewed JASA publication of the same study (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade 2008, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114-1125, PubMed 18247912): "Two experiments measured babble signal-to-noise ratio thresholds for fricative minimal pair distinctions for 14 normal-hearing listeners and 14 listeners with simulated sloping, recruiting impairment. Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall." The study is explicitly about fricative consonants. MINOR QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): the abstract notes "for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were not found for non-sibilant pairs" — i.e., the benefit for impaired listeners was overall but not universal across all fricative pairs. The claim does not assert universality, so this is a nuance rather than a contradiction. Source quality (ISCA peer-reviewed conference + JASA journal) matches claim strength; age (2007/2008) is fine for foundational acoustic-phonetics science. Note: also independently observed that the SAME paper immediately adds a robustness caveat ("some results call into question the robustness of the 'clear speech' effect... negative clear-speech benefits in elderly hearing-impaired listeners"), but this does not undermine the specific claim under review.

content.text: Verified the claim against the primary source and an independent peer-reviewed confirmation. The claim is well-supported and accurately quoted. refuted=false with high confidence.
